================================================================================
  VANHOOKS — COMPLETE TECHNICAL SKILL REFERENCE
  Version sourced from: VanHooks v0.1.0 
  C++ Standard: C++23 throughout
================================================================================

This document is a complete technical reference for VanHooks.
It is written to be fully self-contained — no source files need to be uploaded
for Claude to work with either library correctly.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PART 1 — VANHOOKS
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1.1  WHAT VANHOOKS IS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

VanHooks is a C++23 cross-platform function hooking library. It is a complete
replacement for MinHook, EasyHook, SafetyHook, and PolyHook2 with the following
advantages:

  Platform support : Windows, Linux, macOS
  Architecture     : x86, x64, ARM64
  Hook types       : Trampoline, IAT, PLT/GOT, VTable, Mid-function
  Error handling   : std::expected<T, Error> — no exceptions, no global state
  Disassembler     : Zydis (fetched automatically via CMake FetchContent)
  C++ standard     : C++23 (std::expected, std::span, designated initializers)

Comparison table (from README):
  MinHook    — Win only, no ARM64, IAT only, C enum errors, C89 API
  EasyHook   — Win only, no ARM64, IAT only, C enum errors, .NET
  SafetyHook — Win/Lin, no ARM64, no table hooks, exceptions, C++23
  PolyHook2  — Win only, no ARM64, IAT+VTable, exceptions, C++20
  VanHooks   — Win/Lin/macOS, ARM64, all hook types, std::expected, C++23

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1.2  HEADER LAYOUT
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

  include/vanhooks/
    vanhooks.hpp      — Core types, Engine API, all public structs and enums
    memory.hpp        — Memory protection, allocation, ScopedWritable, TrampolineAllocator
    disasm.hpp        — Disassembler interface (Zydis x64 + ARM64 manual decoder)
    trampoline.hpp    — Patch builder, jump encoding, ThreadFence, PatchContext
    tables.hpp        — IAT / PLT / GOT / VTable hook internals

  src/
    engine.cpp        — Central hook registry and lifecycle manager
    disasm.cpp        — Zydis x64 backend + ARM64 fixed-width decoder
    trampoline.cpp    — x64 + ARM64 patch builder + apply/remove
    thread_fence.cpp  — Thread suspension (NtGetNextThread / procfs / task_threads)
    memory_win.cpp    — VirtualAlloc, VirtualProtect, near-target probe (Windows)
    memory_posix.cpp  — mmap, mprotect, /proc/self/maps, dl_iterate_phdr (Linux/macOS)
    tables_win.cpp    — PE IAT walk, EAT, VTable patching (Windows)
    tables_posix.cpp  — GOT (Linux), lazy pointers (macOS), VTable
    obfstr.hpp        — Compile-time XOR string obfuscation (internal, not public API)

The only header you #include in user code is:
    #include <vanhooks/vanhooks.hpp>

All other headers are consumed internally. You do not include memory.hpp,
disasm.hpp, trampoline.hpp, or tables.hpp in application code.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1.3  CMAKE INTEGRATION
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Option A — FetchContent (recommended, Zydis fetched automatically):

    include(FetchContent)
    FetchContent_Declare(vanhooks
        GIT_REPOSITORY https://github.com/your-org/vanhooks.git
        GIT_TAG        main
    )
    FetchContent_MakeAvailable(vanhooks)
    target_link_libraries(my_target PRIVATE VanHooks::vanhooks)

Option B — Drop-in precompiled lib (like MinHook):

    if(CMAKE_SIZEOF_VOID_P EQUAL 8)
        set(VH_LIB_DIR "${CMAKE_CURRENT_SOURCE_DIR}/lib/win-x64")
    else()
        set(VH_LIB_DIR "${CMAKE_CURRENT_SOURCE_DIR}/lib/win-x86")
    endif()

    add_library(VanHooks::vanhooks STATIC IMPORTED)
    set_target_properties(VanHooks::vanhooks PROPERTIES
        IMPORTED_LOCATION_RELEASE "${VH_LIB_DIR}/Release/vanhooks.lib"
        IMPORTED_LOCATION_DEBUG   "${VH_LIB_DIR}/Debug/vanhooks.lib"
        INTERFACE_INCLUDE_DIRECTORIES "${CMAKE_CURRENT_SOURCE_DIR}/include"
    )
    target_link_libraries(my_target PRIVATE VanHooks::vanhooks)

All libs use static CRT (/MT or /MTd). No VC Redist dependency.
CMake minimum version required: 3.25.
Compiler requirements: MSVC 19.38+, GCC 13+, Clang 17+.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1.4  ERROR TYPE SYSTEM
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Every VanHooks operation returns Result<T> or Status:

    template<typename T>
    using Result = std::expected<T, Error>;
    using Status = Result<void>;

Error enum (complete list from vanhooks.hpp):

    // General
    Ok = 0, NotInitialized, AlreadyInitialized, InvalidArgument,
    Unsupported, OutOfRange, OsError,

    // Memory (100–104)
    MemoryAllocationFailed, MemoryProtectFailed, MemoryReadFailed,
    MemoryWriteFailed, TrampolineNoSpace,

    // Hook lifecycle (200–205)
    HookNotFound, HookAlreadyExists, HookInstallFailed, HookRemoveFailed,
    HookAlreadyEnabled, HookAlreadyDisabled,

    // Disassembly (300–302)
    DisassemblyFailed, InstructionTooShort, UnsupportedInstruction,

    // Module / symbol (400–403)
    ModuleNotFound, SymbolNotFound, IATEntryNotFound, PLTEntryNotFound,

    // Thread (500–502)
    ThreadSuspendFailed, ThreadResumeFailed, ThreadInPrologue,

    // Batch / chain (600–602)
    QueueEmpty, ChainBaseNotFound, ChainOrderViolation

Convert to string:
    std::string_view vanhooks::error_to_string(Error e) noexcept;

Pattern for checking results:
    auto r = eng.hook(&fn, &detour, &orig);
    if (!r) {
        printf("failed: %s\n", vanhooks::error_to_string(r.error()).data());
        return;
    }
    // r.value() is the HookHandle

Chaining with and_then / transform_error:
    auto r = eng.hook(&fn, &detour, &orig)
        .and_then([&](auto h) { return eng.enable(h); })
        .transform_error([](vanhooks::Error e) {
            fprintf(stderr, "VanHooks: %s\n",
                vanhooks::error_to_string(e).data());
            return e;
        });

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1.5  HOOKHANDLE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

    struct HookHandle {
        uint64_t id = 0;
        bool valid() const noexcept { return id != 0; }
        explicit operator bool() const noexcept { return valid(); }
    };

Every successful hook installation returns a HookHandle. Store it to enable,
disable, or remove the hook later. An invalid handle has id == 0.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1.6  HOOKDESC — INTROSPECTION
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

    struct HookDesc {
        HookHandle  handle;
        HookKind    kind;       // Trampoline / IAT / PLT / VTable / Mid
        void*       target;     // original function address
        void*       detour;     // replacement function address
        void*       trampoline; // callable original (trampoline hooks only)
        bool        enabled;
        std::string tag;        // optional human-readable label
    };

Retrieve via:
    std::optional<HookDesc>  eng.describe(HookHandle h) const;
    std::vector<HookDesc>    eng.list_hooks() const;
    size_t                   eng.hook_count() const noexcept;
    HookHandle               eng.find_by_target(const void* target) const noexcept;

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1.7  ENGINE CLASS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Engine is the central object. All hooks are registered in it.
There is also a process-wide global engine accessed via global_engine().

    class Engine {
    public:
        struct Config {
            std::shared_ptr<IAllocator> allocator;         // null = default
            size_t   trampoline_pool_size  = 65536;        // bytes per slab
            bool     auto_flush_icache     = true;
            bool     enable_integrity_watchdog = false;
            uint32_t watchdog_interval_ms      = 500;
            bool     suppress_etw              = false;    // Windows only
            bool     suppress_amsi             = false;    // Windows only
        };

        explicit Engine(Config cfg = {});
        ~Engine();  // calls remove_all() automatically
        // non-copyable, movable
    };

    // Global engine (zero setup):
    Engine& vanhooks::global_engine();

Config fields in full detail:

  trampoline_pool_size
    Bytes per TrampolineAllocator slab. Default 65536. Each slab holds
    65536 / 128 = 512 trampoline slots. Increase only if hooking hundreds
    of functions simultaneously.

  auto_flush_icache
    Calls flush_icache() after every patch write. Should always be true
    on ARM64. Safe to leave true on x64 (is a no-op there internally).

  enable_integrity_watchdog
    Spawns a background thread (via NtCreateThreadEx on Windows, std::thread
    on POSIX) that wakes every watchdog_interval_ms milliseconds and
    memcmp's each enabled trampoline hook's live bytes against the cached
    hook_patch bytes. If they differ (removed by an external driver), it
    reinstalls the patch. Thread is created via NtCreateThreadEx so
    CreateThread does not appear in the IAT.

  suppress_etw (Windows only, default false)
    Patches ntdll!EtwEventWrite and ntdll!EtwEventWriteFull to execute
    XOR EAX,EAX; RET immediately. Silences user-mode ETW providers.
    No-op on HVCI/VBS systems where ntdll .text is hypervisor-locked.

  suppress_amsi (Windows only, default false)
    Uses VanHooks' own trampoline hook to intercept amsi.dll!AmsiScanBuffer
    and amsi.dll!AmsiScanString, writing AMSI_RESULT_CLEAN to the result
    out-parameter and returning S_OK. No-op if amsi.dll is not loaded.
    Uses proper parameter ABI (not hardcoded RSP offsets).

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1.8  TRAMPOLINE HOOKS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Trampoline hooks overwrite the first N bytes of a target function with a
jump to the detour. A trampoline stub in separately allocated executable
memory holds the displaced original bytes followed by a jump back, making
the original callable through the stored function pointer.

Patch strategies by architecture:
  x86   : 5-byte rel32 JMP (always fits, all addresses within ±2GB)
  x64   : 5-byte rel32 JMP if target within ±2GB of detour,
           else 14-byte FF25 indirect JMP [RIP+0] with 8-byte address pool
           (no register clobber)
  ARM64 : 4-byte B #offset if within ±128MB,
           else 20-byte ADRP+LDR+BR or MOVZ/MOVK×4+BR (20 bytes, anywhere)

Trampoline slot size: 128 bytes (ARM64 worst case = 5 stolen insns × 20 bytes
+ 20-byte jump-back; x64 worst case = 4 insns × 15 bytes + 14-byte jump-back).

Basic typed hook (enforces function signature at compile time):
    static RetType (*orig_fn)(Args...) = nullptr;

    RetType detour_fn(Args... args) {
        // pre-call logic
        auto result = orig_fn(args...);
        // post-call logic
        return result;
    }

    auto r = eng.hook(&target_fn, &detour_fn, &orig_fn);
    // or using global engine shorthand:
    auto r = vanhooks::hook(&target_fn, &detour_fn, &orig_fn);

Untyped (void*) hook — use when you have raw addresses:
    Result<HookHandle> eng.hook_trampoline(
        void*             target,
        void*             detour,
        void**            original_out = nullptr,
        TrampolineOptions opts         = {}
    );

TrampolineOptions struct:
    struct TrampolineOptions {
        bool        thread_safe = true;  // suspend threads during patch
        std::string tag;                 // human-readable label for describe()
    };

  thread_safe = true  (default):
    Constructs a ThreadFence that enumerates and suspends all threads via
    NtGetNextThread (Windows), /proc/self/task (Linux), or task_threads
    (macOS). Applies the patch. Resumes all threads. Use when you cannot
    guarantee where other threads are executing.

  thread_safe = false:
    No thread suspension. Only safe when you know the other threads are NOT
    executing at or near the target function — for example when calling from
    inside a Present() hook where you know the main thread is in Present.

Hook by module + symbol name (equivalent to MH_CreateHookApiEx):
    Result<HookHandle> eng.hook_api(
        std::string_view  module_name,    // e.g. "user32.dll" or "user32"
        std::string_view  function_name,  // e.g. "MessageBoxW"
        void*             detour,
        void**            original_out = nullptr,
        TrampolineOptions opts         = {}
    );

    // Typed variant:
    Result<HookHandle> eng.hook_api<PFN>(
        std::string_view module_name,
        std::string_view function_name,
        PFN*             detour,
        PFN**            original_out = nullptr,
        TrampolineOptions opts        = {}
    );

    // Resolves target via GetModuleHandleA + GetProcAddress (Windows)
    // or dlsym(RTLD_DEFAULT, ...) (POSIX), then calls hook_trampoline().

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1.9  IAT HOOKS (Windows only)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

IAT hooks redirect calls within a specific module's Import Address Table
without touching the target function's bytes. Safe when the target function
prologue is too short for a trampoline (HookInstallFailed).

Patch first matching IAT slot:
    Result<HookHandle> eng.hook_iat(
        void*       detour,
        IATOptions  opts
    );

Patch ALL matching IAT slots across all loaded modules:
    Result<std::vector<HookHandle>> eng.hook_iat_all(
        void*       detour,
        IATOptions  opts
    );

IATOptions struct:
    struct IATOptions {
        std::string module_name;  // which module's IAT to search
                                  // (empty = search all loaded modules)
        std::string import_name;  // symbol name to redirect, e.g. "MessageBoxW"
        std::string tag;
    };

Example:
    static PFN_MessageBoxW orig_mbw = nullptr;
    BOOL WINAPI hk_mbw(HWND h, LPCWSTR text, LPCWSTR cap, UINT t) {
        return orig_mbw(h, L"[intercepted]", cap, t);
    }

    // Patch just the IAT slot in "myapp.exe"
    auto r = eng.hook_iat(
        (void*)&hk_mbw,
        vanhooks::IATOptions{ .import_name = "MessageBoxW",
                               .module_name = "myapp.exe" }
    );

    // Patch every IAT slot for malloc across all loaded modules
    auto rs = eng.hook_iat_all(
        (void*)&hk_malloc,
        vanhooks::IATOptions{ .import_name = "malloc" }
    );

Internal mechanism: walks the PE IMAGE_IMPORT_DESCRIPTOR chain of the
target module, finds slots matching import_name, calls VirtualProtect to
PAGE_READWRITE, writes the detour pointer, restores protection.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1.10  PLT/GOT HOOKS (Linux / macOS)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

On Linux, VanHooks patches the GOT entry read by the PLT stub.
On macOS, VanHooks patches the dyld lazy pointer table.

    Result<HookHandle> eng.hook_plt(
        void*       detour,
        PLTOptions  opts
    );

PLTOptions struct:
    struct PLTOptions {
        std::string library_name;  // e.g. "libc" or "libc.so.6"
        std::string symbol_name;   // e.g. "malloc"
        std::string tag;
    };

Linux internal: uses dl_iterate_phdr to walk loaded shared objects,
finds the PT_DYNAMIC segment, locates DT_JMPREL (PLT relocations) and
DT_SYMTAB, matches the symbol name, patches the GOT slot with mprotect.

macOS internal: walks the Mach-O __DATA,__la_symbol_ptr section to find
lazy pointer entries, patches with mprotect.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1.11  VTABLE HOOKS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

VTable hooks patch a single virtual function pointer in a C++ vtable
(or a COM interface vtable). No function bytes are modified.

    Result<HookHandle> eng.hook_vtable(
        void*          detour,
        VTableOptions  opts
    );

VTableOptions struct:
    struct VTableOptions {
        void**      vtable;      // pointer to the vtable array
        size_t      slot_index;  // which slot to patch (0-indexed)
        std::string tag;
    };

Getting the vtable pointer from a live COM object:
    void** vtbl = *reinterpret_cast<void***>(pComObject);
    // vtbl[0] = QueryInterface, vtbl[1] = AddRef, vtbl[2] = Release, ...

Getting the vtable pointer from a live C++ object:
    void** vtbl = *reinterpret_cast<void***>(pObject);
    // equivalent to vanhooks::tables::vtable_of(pObject)

Helper in tables.hpp (available but not required):
    inline void** vanhooks::tables::vtable_of(const void* object) noexcept {
        return *reinterpret_cast<void***>(const_cast<void*>(object));
    }

Example — hooking IDirect3DDevice9::Present (vtable slot 17):
    using PFN_Present = HRESULT(STDMETHODCALLTYPE*)(
        IDirect3DDevice9*, CONST RECT*, CONST RECT*, HWND, CONST RGNDATA*);

    static PFN_Present orig_Present = nullptr;

    HRESULT STDMETHODCALLTYPE hk_Present(
        IDirect3DDevice9* dev,
        CONST RECT* pSrc, CONST RECT* pDst,
        HWND hWnd, CONST RGNDATA* pDirty)
    {
        // overlay logic here
        return orig_Present(dev, pSrc, pDst, hWnd, pDirty);
    }

    void** vtbl = *reinterpret_cast<void***>(pDevice);
    orig_Present = reinterpret_cast<PFN_Present>(vtbl[17]);

    auto r = eng.hook_vtable(
        (void*)&hk_Present,
        vanhooks::VTableOptions{ .vtable = vtbl, .slot_index = 17,
                                  .tag = "Present" }
    );

Known IDirect3DDevice9 vtable slot indices (D3D9 SDK standard):
    0  = QueryInterface       17 = Present
    1  = AddRef               18 = GetBackBuffer
    2  = Release              19 = GetRasterStatus
    3  = TestCooperativeLevel 20 = SetDialogBoxMode
    4  = GetAvailableTextureMem  21 = SetGammaRamp
    5  = EvictManagedResources   22 = GetGammaRamp
    6  = GetDirect3D          23 = CreateTexture
    7  = GetDeviceCaps        ...
    8  = GetDisplayMode       36 = BeginScene
    9  = GetCreationParameters 37 = EndScene
    10 = SetCursorProperties  38 = Clear
    11 = SetCursorPosition    39 = SetTransform
    12 = ShowCursor           40 = GetTransform
    13 = CreateAdditionalSwapChain  41 = MultiplyTransform
    14 = GetSwapChain         42 = SetViewport
    15 = GetNumberOfSwapChains 43 = GetViewport
    16 = Reset                ...

Internal mechanism: calls VirtualProtect/mprotect on exactly sizeof(void*)
bytes at &vtable[slot_index] (not from the vtable base — avoids the common
bug of protecting index*sizeof bytes from the base which may cross a page
boundary). Writes detour pointer. Restores protection. Returns original value.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1.12  MID-FUNCTION HOOKS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Mid-function hooks insert a callback at an arbitrary instruction boundary
inside a function body. The original instructions still execute after the
callback returns. The callback receives a MidContext* with all GPRs and
flags at the hook site. This is VanHooks' primary differentiator from MinHook.

    Result<HookHandle> eng.hook_mid(
        void*             target,        // function start address
        size_t            byte_offset,   // requested byte offset into function
        MidCallback       callback,      // void(*)(MidContext*) noexcept
        TrampolineOptions opts = {}
    );

byte_offset is rounded UP to the nearest complete instruction boundary.
On ARM64 this is (byte_offset + 3) & ~3.
On x86/x64 the disassembler decodes from offset 0 until the accumulated
byte count reaches or exceeds byte_offset, then returns that count.

MidCallback type:
    using MidCallback = void (*)(MidContext*) noexcept;
    // Must be noexcept — runs with a modified stack frame.
    // Must not throw. Must not call back into hooks that acquire VanHooks mutex.

MidContext struct (full layout, x64):
    struct alignas(8) MidContext {
        uint64_t r15, r14, r13, r12, r11, r10, r9, r8;
        uint64_t rdi, rsi, rbp, rbx, rdx, rcx, rax;
        uint64_t rflags;   // saved by PUSHFQ, restored by POPFQ
    };

MidContext struct (x86):
    struct alignas(8) MidContext {
        uint32_t edi, esi, ebp, ebx, edx, ecx, eax;
        uint32_t eflags;
    };

MidContext struct (ARM64):
    struct alignas(8) MidContext {
        uint64_t x[8];   // x0..x7, offsets 0..56
        uint64_t lr;     // x30, offset 64
        uint64_t _pad;   // alignment, offset 72
    };

Trampoline stub layout (x64 Windows, ~83 bytes total):
    PUSHFQ
    PUSH rax, rcx, rdx, rbx, rbp, rsi, rdi, r8..r15  (15 pushes = 120 bytes on stack)
    SUB  RSP, 8                                         (align to 16)
    LEA  RCX, [RSP+8]                                   (MidContext* arg, Windows ABI)
    SUB  RSP, 32                                        (shadow space, Windows only)
    MOV  RAX, <callback address>
    CALL RAX
    ADD  RSP, 32                                        (Windows only)
    ADD  RSP, 8
    POP  r15..r8, rdi, rsi, rbp, rbx, rdx, rcx, rax
    POPFQ
    [relocated stolen bytes]
    JMP  <target + patch_offset + stolen_byte_count>

Example — observe register state at a specific point in a function:
    static void on_tick(vanhooks::MidContext* ctx) noexcept {
        // read rax at this instruction:
        uint64_t val = ctx->rax;
        // modify rdx:
        ctx->rdx = 0x12345678;
        // original instructions still execute after return
    }

    eng.hook_mid(fn_ptr, 0, on_tick);            // at function entry
    eng.hook_mid(fn_ptr, 24, on_tick);           // 24 bytes in (rounded to boundary)

No original-out pointer is provided for mid hooks — they observe/modify
register state, they do not redirect control flow.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1.13  LIFECYCLE OPERATIONS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Per-hook:
    Status eng.enable (HookHandle h);   // re-apply cached patch bytes
    Status eng.disable(HookHandle h);   // write back saved original bytes
    Status eng.remove (HookHandle h);   // disable + free trampoline slot

Bulk:
    Status eng.enable_all ();
    Status eng.disable_all();
    Status eng.remove_all ();

remove() refuses to remove a hook that has chain children registered on it.
You must remove chain links in reverse order before removing the base.

Error codes to expect:
    HookNotFound        — handle was already removed or never existed
    HookAlreadyEnabled  — called enable() on an already-enabled hook
    HookAlreadyDisabled — called disable() on an already-disabled hook
    ChainOrderViolation — tried to remove a base hook before its chain links

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1.14  BATCH QUEUE API
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Installing many hooks individually suspends and resumes all threads once
per hook (when thread_safe=true). The batch queue amortizes this cost:

    void   eng.queue_enable (HookHandle h);
    void   eng.queue_disable(HookHandle h);
    void   eng.queue_remove (HookHandle h);
    Status eng.apply_queued();            // one suspend/resume cycle
    void   eng.clear_queue() noexcept;
    size_t eng.queue_size()  const noexcept;

apply_queued():
  1. Snapshots the queue under the mutex.
  2. Creates one ThreadFence (suspends all threads).
  3. Applies all queued operations under the mutex while fence is held.
  4. ThreadFence destructs (resumes all threads).

Example:
    eng.queue_enable(h1);
    eng.queue_enable(h2);
    eng.queue_disable(h3);
    eng.queue_remove(h4);
    auto r = eng.apply_queued();  // one suspension, all ops atomic
    if (!r && r.error() != vanhooks::Error::QueueEmpty) { /* handle */ }

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1.15  HOOK CHAINING
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Chain a second (or third) detour onto an already-hooked target:

    Result<HookHandle> eng.chain(
        HookHandle   base,
        void*        next_detour,
        void**       next_original_out = nullptr,
        std::string  tag = {}
    );

Call chain with two detours:
    target → detour2 → detour1 → original

next_original_out receives the previous detour's address (detour1), NOT the
trampoline. Calling it runs: detour1 → trampoline → real original.

Removal MUST be in reverse install order:
    eng.remove(link);   // link before base — ChainOrderViolation otherwise
    eng.remove(base);

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1.16  THREADFENCE — INTERNAL DETAIL
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

ThreadFence is in trampoline.hpp. You do not normally use it directly —
it is created automatically by VanHooks when thread_safe=true. Documented
here for completeness and for use in advanced apply_queued() scenarios.

    class ThreadFence {
    public:
        ThreadFence();          // suspends all threads except caller
        ~ThreadFence();         // resumes all suspended threads

        bool  ok()                   const noexcept;
        Error error()                const noexcept;
        size_t suspended_count()     const noexcept;

        bool ip_in_range(uint64_t range_start, size_t range_bytes) const noexcept;

        void fix_ip_range(uint64_t from_start, size_t from_bytes,
                          uint64_t to_addr) noexcept;
    };

ip_fixup_on_remove (Engine member, default true):
    When removing a trampoline hook, if a suspended thread's instruction
    pointer lands inside the stolen prologue bytes, fix_ip_range advances
    it to target + stolen_byte_count so execution continues correctly
    in the restored original. Only effective when thread_safe=true.

Platform details:
  Windows : NtGetNextThread loop
  Linux   : /proc/self/task directory iteration + PTRACE_ATTACH or tgkill
  macOS   : task_threads() Mach API

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1.17  MEMORY SUBSYSTEM — INTERNAL DETAIL
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

memory.hpp — namespace vanhooks::memory

ScopedWritable:
    RAII guard that temporarily makes a region writable and restores
    protection on destruction. Used internally for all vtable and
    trampoline writes. Never produces RWX pages — regions are RW during
    the write, then restored (or explicitly flipped to RX via mark_exec()).

    memory::ScopedWritable guard(addr, size);
    if (!guard.ok()) return std::unexpected(guard.error());
    std::memcpy(addr, patch.data(), patch.size());
    // protection restored by guard destructor

mark_exec(void* addr, size_t size):
    Flips a region from RW to PAGE_EXECUTE_READ (Windows) or PROT_READ|PROT_EXEC
    (POSIX). Must be called after writing trampoline code into a slot.
    Returns MemoryProtectFailed if the OS rejects it (e.g. ACG on anonymous memory).

flush_icache(void* addr, size_t size):
    Flushes the instruction cache. Required on ARM64. No-op on x86/x64
    (cache coherent on all x86 CPUs). Always safe to call on any platform.

TrampolineAllocator:
    Allocates 128-byte slots in slabs positioned near the target address
    so that rel32 JMP encoding (±2GB) is always possible on x64.
    Falls back to absolute encoding automatically when near allocation fails.
    SLOT_SIZE = 128 bytes (ARM64 worst case).

    On Windows x64, also probes code caves in nearby loaded DLLs for slot
    allocation when VirtualAlloc near-target probing fails. Each cave slot
    stores original_bytes for restoration and an on_free callback.

Protect enum:
    None, Read, Write, Execute, ReadWrite, ReadExec, ReadWriteExec

within_rel32(from, to):
    Returns true if (to - from) fits in int32_t range (±2GB).
    Always returns true on 32-bit platforms.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1.18  DISASSEMBLER — INTERNAL DETAIL
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

disasm.hpp — namespace vanhooks::disasm

Backend: Zydis for x86/x64. ARM64 uses a manual fixed-width 4-byte decoder.

Arch enum: X86, X64, ARM64, Unknown
detect_arch() — constexpr, detects at compile time from predefined macros.

InsnKind enum:
    Generic, Call, Jump, JumpConditional, Return, Nop,
    RipRelative, PcRelative, Privileged

Instruction struct:
    uint64_t    address
    uint8_t     length          (1-15 x86/x64, always 4 ARM64)
    InsnKind    kind
    uint8_t     bytes[16]
    optional<uint64_t> absolute_target  (for RIP/PC-relative instructions)
    string      mnemonic        (debug builds)

    bool is_branch()   — Jump, JumpConditional, or Call
    bool is_terminal() — Jump or Return
    bool is_relative() — RipRelative or PcRelative

Disassembler methods:
    Result<Instruction>              decode_one(span, runtime_address)
    Result<vector<Instruction>>      decode_min_bytes(span, runtime_address, min_bytes)
    static size_t                    total_length(vector<Instruction>)

find_safe_patch_point(code, runtime_address, byte_offset, arch):
    Returns the byte offset of the nearest instruction boundary at or after
    byte_offset. Never splits an instruction. Used by hook_mid().

rewrite_for_relocation(insns, original_base, new_base, arch):
    Returns rewritten bytes with RIP/PC-relative references fixed up for
    the new location. Used by the trampoline builder to relocate stolen bytes.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1.19  STRING OBFUSCATION — OBFSTR.HPP
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

obfstr.hpp is internal to VanHooks (in src/, not include/).
Documented here because it is used for all sensitive string literals
inside engine.cpp (ntdll.dll, NtCreateThreadEx, amsi.dll, etc.).

Mechanism: compile-time XOR with per-position key:
    key(pos, len) = 0x4E ^ (pos * 0x1B) ^ (len * 0x37) ^ ((pos>>2) * 0x5D)
Plaintext strings never appear in .rdata — only XOR'd bytes are stored.

Usage pattern (internal to VanHooks, not for application code):
    static constexpr ObfStr enc_ntdll("ntdll.dll");
    char buf[enc_ntdll.size()]{};
    enc_ntdll.decode(buf);
    // use buf
    SecureZeroMemory(buf, sizeof(buf));

Macro shorthand used in engine.cpp:
    VH_DECODE(var, obf_str)
    // expands to: char var[obf_str.size()]{}; obf_str.decode(var);

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1.20  MIGRATING FROM MINHOOK
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

MinHook API → VanHooks API mapping:

    MH_Initialize()                → not needed (global_engine() ready on first use)
    MH_Uninitialize()              → not needed (Engine destructor calls remove_all())
    MH_CreateHook(target, det, &orig) → eng.hook_trampoline(target, det, &orig)
    MH_CreateHookApi("mod","fn",det,&orig) → eng.hook_api("mod","fn",det,&orig)
    MH_EnableHook(MH_ALL_HOOKS)    → eng.enable_all()
    MH_DisableHook(MH_ALL_HOOKS)   → eng.disable_all()
    MH_EnableHook(target)          → eng.enable(handle)
    MH_DisableHook(target)         → eng.disable(handle)
    MH_RemoveHook(target)          → eng.remove(handle)
    MH_QueueEnableHook(target)     → eng.queue_enable(handle)
    MH_QueueDisableHook(target)    → eng.queue_disable(handle)
    MH_ApplyQueued()               → eng.apply_queued()
    MH_StatusToString(st)          → vanhooks::error_to_string(e)

Key behavioral differences:
    - VanHooks tracks hooks by HookHandle, not by target address.
      Keep the returned HookHandle from every hook() call.
    - The typed hook<Fn>() template catches signature mismatches at compile
      time. MinHook uses LPVOID everywhere — mismatches are runtime crashes.
    - No MH_Initialize / MH_Uninitialize lifecycle to manage.
    - enable_all() / disable_all() hold the mutex for the entire operation,
      eliminating TOCTOU windows that MinHook's per-hook locking had.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1.21  CRITICAL RULE — HOOK INSTALLATION TIMING
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

NEVER install hooks from DllMain or from any thread that holds the loader lock.

VirtualProtect (called internally) acquires the memory manager lock.
If any suspended thread holds that lock (common during DLL init), the
hook thread deadlocks permanently.

WRONG pattern:
    case DLL_PROCESS_ATTACH:
        Sleep(1000);          // still racy
        InstallHooks();       // deadlocks if main thread holds D3D/loader locks

CORRECT pattern — install from inside the first Present() call:
    HRESULT STDMETHODCALLTYPE hk_Present(...) {
        static std::once_flag once;
        std::call_once(once, []() {
            // Main thread is inside Present — not in any hook site.
            // Safe to install with thread_safe=false.
            vanhooks::TrampolineOptions opts{ .thread_safe = false };
            eng.hook_mid(addr, 0, callback, opts);
        });
        return orig_Present(...);
    }

When to use thread_safe=false:
    You know exactly where the main thread is (inside Present, EndScene, etc.)
    and it is NOT at any of the hook sites. No thread suspension overhead.

When to use thread_safe=true (default):
    Installing from a generic background thread where you cannot guarantee
    the main thread is away from the hook sites.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1.22  REENTRANCY GUARD
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Detours that call back into hooked functions need a per-thread guard:

    thread_local bool tls_in_hook = false;

    int detour_fn(int x) {
        if (tls_in_hook) return orig_fn(x);  // bypass — already in hook
        tls_in_hook = true;
        int result = orig_fn(x);
        tls_in_hook = false;
        return result;
    }

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1.23  COMMON FAILURE MODES
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

HookInstallFailed
    Target function prologue is too short for a trampoline patch.
    Use IAT hook instead.

MemoryAllocationFailed
    No executable memory could be allocated near the target within ±2GB.
    Increase trampoline_pool_size or use force_abs_jump option.

InstructionTooShort
    Disassembler cannot safely steal enough bytes (function body too small).
    Use IAT hook or VTable hook instead.

ThreadSuspendFailed
    Thread enumeration failed. Possible causes: dying process, HVCI active,
    or insufficient privileges. Use thread_safe=false from a controlled context.

ThreadInPrologue
    A suspended thread's IP is inside the stolen bytes. VanHooks will attempt
    ip_fixup if ip_fixup_on_remove=true. If it returns this error, retry.

HookAlreadyExists
    Tried to hook the same target address twice via hook_trampoline().
    Use eng.chain() to add a second detour instead.

ChainOrderViolation
    Tried to remove a base hook before removing its chain links.
    Remove in reverse install order.

Hook silently disappears at runtime
    A kernel-mode driver restored the original bytes.
    Enable Config::enable_integrity_watchdog.

Game crashes on hook install (not on first frame)
    Installing from DllMain or a timed background thread that fires while
    the main thread holds D3D or loader locks. Install from Present() instead.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1.24  COMPLETE USAGE EXAMPLE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

    #include <vanhooks/vanhooks.hpp>

    // --- trampoline hook (typed) ---
    static int (*orig_add)(int, int) = nullptr;

    int detour_add(int a, int b) {
        printf("add(%d, %d)\n", a, b);
        return orig_add(a, b);
    }

    // --- vtable hook ---
    using PFN_Present = HRESULT(STDMETHODCALLTYPE*)(
        IDirect3DDevice9*, CONST RECT*, CONST RECT*, HWND, CONST RGNDATA*);
    static PFN_Present orig_Present = nullptr;

    HRESULT STDMETHODCALLTYPE hk_Present(
        IDirect3DDevice9* dev,
        CONST RECT* pSrc, CONST RECT* pDst,
        HWND hWnd, CONST RGNDATA* pDirty)
    {
        static std::once_flag once;
        std::call_once(once, [dev]() {
            // Safe install from first Present() — thread_safe=false
            vanhooks::TrampolineOptions opts{ .thread_safe = false,
                                              .tag = "add_hook" };
            vanhooks::global_engine().hook(
                &add, &detour_add, &orig_add, opts);
        });
        return orig_Present(dev, pSrc, pDst, hWnd, pDirty);
    }

    void install_present_hook(IDirect3DDevice9* pDevice) {
        void** vtbl = *reinterpret_cast<void***>(pDevice);
        orig_Present = reinterpret_cast<PFN_Present>(vtbl[17]);

        auto r = vanhooks::global_engine().hook_vtable(
            (void*)&hk_Present,
            vanhooks::VTableOptions{ .vtable = vtbl,
                                      .slot_index = 17,
                                      .tag = "Present" });
        if (!r)
            printf("hook_vtable failed: %s\n",
                vanhooks::error_to_string(r.error()).data());
    }

    void teardown() {
        vanhooks::global_engine().remove_all();
    }
